1: ’Ahar ken (after this) marks the pivot from prose prologue to poetic dialogue. Patah ’iyyov ’et-pihu (Job opened his mouth) — the formal introduction of direct speech, used for significant utterances (cf. Balaam’s oracles, Num 22:28; 23:5). Vayeqallel ’et-yomo (he cursed his day) — the verb qalal in the pi’el (intensive stem) = curse thoroughly, call down misfortune. This is not barakh (the adversary’s predicted curse-word) but qalal — Job curses the day, not God. The distinction is precise and legally significant within the book’s framing.
2: The transition formula vaya’an ’iyyov vayyomar (Job responded/spoke and said) introduces the poetic speech. ’Anah in the qal = to respond/answer, even when addressing no prior speaker. Here Job responds to his own silence, opening what will become the book’s central 35-chapter dialogue. The formal speech introduction distinguishes the poetic speeches from the prose narrative, marking the generic shift.
3: Yo’vad yom (let the day perish) — the jussive (yo’vad) marks the curse: let it perish. The jussive forms (yo’vad, v3; yehi hoshekh, v4; yig’alehu, v5; yiqahenu, v6) carry the chapter’s curse-prayer syntax throughout. Gavar zakhar (a male was conceived/was strong) — the root gavar = to be strong, prevail; its nominal form gever = a strong man (as opposed to ’ish = man of standing, ’adam = humanity, ’enosh = mortal). The announcement “a gaver (strong male) has been conceived” celebrates the birth of one expected to have strength; Job curses that announcement.
4: Haya hahu hoshekh (let that day be darkness) — Job reverses the creation-light of Gen 1:3-4 (yehi ’or, let there be light; vayar ’elohim ki-tov, God saw it was good). Job’s curse inverts the creation sequence: he is uncreating the day of his birth. The phrase ’al-yidrsheihu ’eloah mima’al (may God above not seek/care for it) — God’s providential attention (darash = to inquire/seek) is the source of light; Job prays God would not seek this particular day.
5: Yig’alehu hoshekh vetsalmavet (let darkness and deep shadow reclaim/redeem it) — the verb ga’al = to redeem, to function as next-of-kin redeemer. The bitter irony: ga’al is used throughout the OT for YHWH’s redemptive action (Exod 6:6; Isa 43:1); here Job prays that darkness, not YHWH, would be the go’el (redeemer) of his birthday. Tsalmavet = deep shadow / shadow of death, a compound: tsel (shadow) + mavet (death).
6: Hallaylah hahu yiqqahehu ’ofel (let deep darkness seize that night) — ’ofel = gloom, thick darkness. The night of his conception is to be excluded from the annual count (bal-yihad bimei-shanah = let it not be counted among the days of the year). The complete erasure from the calendar: the day/night of Job’s conception and birth is to be excised from time.
7: Galmud (barren, sterile) — used for the infertile woman in Isa 49:21 and Job 15:34. The night is to be barren: lo tavo renana vo (let no shout of joy enter it). Renana = a shout of joy, ringing cry. Job wishes the night of his conception had been infertile and joyless.
8: ’orere yom (those who curse days) — a reference to professional cursers, specialists in cursing or incantation, associated with astrological practices. Ha-’atidim ’orer livyatan (those who are skilled at rousing Leviathan) — Leviathan is the chaos monster of ancient Near Eastern cosmology (appearing in Job 3:8; 40:25[41:1]; Ps 74:14; 104:26; Isa 27:1). Job calls on the professional cursers to activate the chaos monster against his birthday, placing his lament in a mythological register that goes beyond conventional Hebrew prayer.
9: The wish that the stars of the predawn twilight (kokevei nishpoh) would go dark — nishpoh probably = its twilight/its eyelashes (the stars as the flickering lashes of the dawn). Yequwweh la-’or ve-’ayin (let it wait for light but there be none) — the night of his birth should experience what Job is currently experiencing: waiting for relief that does not come. Ve-’al-yir’eh be’afappei-shahar (and let it not see the dawning of the morning) — ’afappei shahar = the eyelids of the dawn (a striking poetic phrase for the dawn’s first light).
10: The reason for the curse: ki lo sakar dalte bitni (because it did not close the doors of my mother’s womb). The womb-door image: if only the womb-doors had remained closed, the birth prevented. Vataster ’amal me’einai (and did not hide trouble from my eyes) — ’amal = toil, trouble, mischief (the same word used in 4:8). If birth had been prevented, Job would not have seen the trouble (’amal) he now experiences.
11: Lamma’ (why) introduces the first of Job’s three lamma’ questions in this chapter (vv11, 20, 23). Lamma’ in lament poetry is the interrogative of protest rather than inquiry — not seeking information but expressing the unbearability of the situation. Lo ’emut meraham (why did I not die from the womb) — Job regrets surviving birth.
12: Madu’a qiddemonti berakhayim (why were there knees to receive me?) — the knees of the midwife or father receiving the newborn, a gesture of acceptance into the family (cf. Gen 50:23, Jacob’s grandsons placed on Joseph’s knees). The alternative is direct death without acceptance. Shaddayim ki yana’qti (breasts that I nursed) — the second element: why was there milk to sustain the life that Job now wishes had ended at birth?
13: The description of Sheol as a place of rest: az ashkav vashaqot (then I would have lain down and been at rest) — the verbs shakav (lie down) and shaqat (be still/quiet). Yashanti az yanuah li (I would have slept and then rested to myself). The fourfold vocabulary of rest in Sheol (lie down, be still, sleep, rest) forms a picture of peaceful death that Job contrasts with his present suffering.
14: Job imagines being at rest with melakhim ve-yo’atse ’arets (kings and counselors of the earth) who habaneh horavot lamoh (built ruins for themselves) — a puzzling phrase that likely means “who built tombs/monuments for themselves” or “whose great works are now ruins.” The point is egalitarian: in death, Job would rest alongside those who had power and built monuments, all now reduced to silence.
15: ’Osarot levamo millu (who filled their mansions with silver) — the kings and princes who hoarded wealth in life are reduced to the same silence as the poorest. Wealth and power are no armor against Sheol. The kasar (princes/officials) of v15 are rulers; their treasuries are irrelevant in death.
16: Ke-nefel tamun (like a hidden stillborn) — nefel = that which falls out, a stillbirth (Ps 58:8; Eccl 6:3). Job imagines a preferable alternative existence as a stillborn child: never having emerged, never having seen the light. The stillborn is “hidden” (tamun) — buried without ceremony, its brief existence concealed from the world.
17: Sham rasha’im hadlu rogez (there the wicked cease from rogez) — rogez = turmoil, agitation, disturbance. In Sheol, the wicked no longer trouble their victims; the yegi’e koah (those exhausted in strength) find rest. Job’s picture of Sheol in ch3 is notably positive — a place of rest, equality, and peace — contrasting with later passages where Sheol is a place of forgetfulness and darkness.
18: Yishhadu la-’otsar (the prisoners rest together) — all prisoners (’asir) find rest there, no longer hearing the overseer’s voice (qol nogesh). Nogesh = taskmaster, driver (same word as the Egyptian taskmasters of Exod 5:6). The oppressive social structures of the living world cannot reach into Sheol.
19: Qaton vegadol sham hu (the lowly and the great are alike there) — Sheol levels all social distinctions. Ve-’eved hofshi me-’adonav (and the slave is free from his master) — the ultimate social leveling: the slave, who in life is owned and directed, is freed (hofshi = free, freed slave) from the master’s authority by death. This is one of the OT’s most radical statements about death as the equalizer of social hierarchy.
20: Lamah yitten la-’amel ’or (why does he give light to the suffering one?) — the second lamah (why). ’Amel = the one who labors under burden/toils/suffers. The gift of life (light) to those in bitter anguish (mare nefesh = bitter of soul) seems cruel: existence itself is the torture when the sufferer longs for Sheol’s peace.
21: Hameyahhalim lemmavet ve-’einenu (those who long for death that does not come) — yahhal in the pi’el = to wait/long intensely. Death refuses to come to those who seek it most desperately. Vayhapseruhu mimmattamonot (they dig for it more eagerly than for buried treasure) — the metaphor of mining/digging for treasure from 28:1–11 is anticipated here: death is searched for with the intensity that treasure hunters search for gold.
22: Ha-semehim ’ale-gil yasisu ki yimshe’u qaver (those who are overjoyed and exult when they finally reach the grave) — the grave is the longed-for destination; finding it produces joy. This is grief pressed to its extremity: the only good news Job can imagine is death. Qaver = the grave/tomb (as distinct from she’ol, the realm of the dead).
23: The third lamah: le-gever asher darko nistarah (to a man whose way is hidden) — gever = a strong man; his way (derek) is hidden (nistar, from sathar = hide). Vayasek ’eloah ba’ado (and whom God has fenced in on every side) — the verb sakak = to hedge/fence, the same root the adversary used in 1:10 (vayyasek) to describe YHWH’s protective hedge around Job. The adversary saw the hedge as protection; Job now experiences the same enclosure as entrapment. Same word, opposite evaluation.
24: Ki lifnei lhmi ’anhati tavo (for my sighing comes before my food) — ’anhah = sighing/groaning (the involuntary sound of grief, Ps 31:10; 102:5). Job’s grief is prior to and greater than his hunger; the body’s need is subordinated to the soul’s anguish. Vayyittpeku kamayim she’agotai (and my cries pour out like rushing water) — she’agah = a roar/cry of pain; nathakh = to pour out, flow freely.
25: Ki pahad pahad wayyi’teni (what I dreaded most has come upon me) — pahad twice: the noun and the verb from the same root, an emphatic figura etymologica. Job’s fear (pahad) anticipated the catastrophe; the catastrophe arrived. The iterative: the fear itself, not merely the event, is named twice. This verse is sometimes read as a theological problem: Job feared disaster, suggesting his fear was a failure of faith. But the context is lamentation, not confession.
26: Lo shalavti ve-lo shaqatti ve-lo-nahatti vayavo rogez (I had no ease, no rest, no peace, yet trouble came) — three negated nouns of rest: shalav (ease), shaqat (quiet), nuah (rest) — all denied. Then: vayavo rogez (yet rogez came) — rogez is the same word used in v17 for what ceases in Sheol. Job has the opposite of Sheol’s peace: constant agitation (rogez) where peace should be. The chiasm closes ch3: the rogez that will cease in Sheol (v17) is the same rogez that has overtaken Job’s waking life (v26).
